chapter_context.summary: Rebukes the wealthy, luxurious women of Samaria, calling them 'kine of Bashan' who oppress the poor, mocks their hypocritical sacrifices at Bethel and Gilgal, and recounts five historical plagues God sent to warn them, ending with: 'Prepare to meet thy God, O Israel'.

outline.text: Denunciation of the Pampered Elite ('Kine of Bashan') Oppressing the Needy
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Denunciation of the Pampered Elite ('Kine of Bashan') Oppressing the Needy (4:1-3), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Amos.

outline.text: Irony: Inviting the People to Go to Bethel and Gilgal to Multiply Transgressions
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Irony: Inviting the People to Go to Bethel and Gilgal to Multiply Transgressions (4:4-5), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Amos.

outline.text: The Fivefold Warning Plagues (Famine, Drought, Blight, Pestilence, Earthquake) and the Refrain: 'Yet have ye not returned unto me'
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Fivefold Warning Plagues (Famine, Drought, Blight, Pestilence, Earthquake) and the Refrain: 'Yet have ye not returned unto me' (4:6-11), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Amos.

outline.text: The Ultimate Challenge: 'Prepare to meet thy God' and the Hymn to the Creator
outline.explanation: This section focuses on The Ultimate Challenge: 'Prepare to meet thy God' and the Hymn to the Creator (4:12-13), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Amos.

key_themes.title: Social Justice and Equity
key_themes.description: Amos fiercely denounces religious ritual that exists alongside economic exploitation, emphasizing that true worship requires justice for the poor.

key_themes.title: The Danger of Spiritual Complacency
key_themes.description: The book warns that material prosperity can easily blind a nation to its spiritual and ethical decline, leading to swift judgment.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the indictments and visions of Chapter 4, how can you actively champion justice and care for the needy in your community today?
How can we guard ourselves against the kind of complacency and empty religious formalism that Amos rebuked in Samaria?

cultural_historical.title: The Reign of Jeroboam II
cultural_historical.text: Amos prophesied during the mid-8th century BC, a period of remarkable material prosperity, territory expansion, and luxurious stability for the Northern Kingdom under Jeroboam II, which unfortunately saw extreme wealth disparity and systemic social oppression.
